subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: No FLA Property Equalization for Unmarried Partners
fact_pattern: Jessica and Mark have cohabited for 9 years without marrying. They have one child together. They now separate. Jessica contributed significantly to the home (registered solely in Mark's name) by paying renovation costs and acting as the primary caregiver for their child. Jessica asks whether she is entitled to property equalization under the Family Law Act.
call_of_question: Is Jessica entitled to property equalization under Part I of the Family Law Act as a common-law spouse?

options.A: Yes — after 3 years of cohabitation, unmarried spouses acquire the same property equalization rights as married spouses under the FLA.
options.B: Yes — any person who shares a child with another person is entitled to FLA property equalization regardless of whether they were married.
options.C: No — Part I of the Family Law Act (equalization of net family property) applies only to married spouses; Jessica and Mark are unmarried and Jessica has no right to equalization of net family property under the FLA.
options.D: Yes — after 5 years of cohabitation, a common-law spouse is automatically deemed a 'spouse' under all provisions of the FLA, including property equalization.

explanation: Part I of the Family Law Act (equalization of net family property, including the matrimonial home regime) applies exclusively to married spouses. Section 1(1) of the FLA defines 'spouse' for the purposes of Part I to mean only persons who are or were married to each other. Common-law partners — regardless of the length of cohabitation or whether they have children together — have no right to equalization of net family property under the FLA. Jessica must rely on other legal theories (such as unjust enrichment or resulting trust) to assert a claim to an interest in the home.
why_A_wrong: The FLA does not grant property equalization rights to unmarried cohabiting spouses, regardless of the length of cohabitation. The three-year cohabitation threshold in the FLA relates only to support rights (s.29), not property rights.
why_B_wrong: Sharing a child does not confer FLA property equalization rights. The FLA Part I equalization regime applies solely to married spouses. Parenthood is relevant to spousal support (FLA s.29) but not to property equalization.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA Part I property equalization applies only to married spouses; Jessica as a common-law partner has no FLA equalization rights and must pursue other legal remedies.
why_D_wrong: No five-year cohabitation period converts a common-law relationship into a marriage for FLA property equalization purposes. The FLA does not deem cohabiting partners to be 'spouses' for property equalization purposes regardless of cohabitation length.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: No FLA Property Equalization for Unmarried Partners
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 1(1) (definition of spouse — Part I), Part I

tested_concepts: FLA Part I applies only to married spouses

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Unjust Enrichment as Basis for Property Claim by Unmarried Partners
fact_pattern: After 12 years of cohabitation, Sandra and Greg separate. During the relationship, Sandra worked part-time and managed the home and their two children, allowing Greg to advance his career. Greg accumulated significant savings and a business. The home is registered in Greg's name. Sandra now claims an interest in Greg's property.
call_of_question: What is the primary legal basis on which a common-law partner may claim an interest in property held in the other partner's name upon relationship breakdown in Ontario?

options.A: The Family Law Act — Part I equalization applies equally to all couples who have cohabited for 12 or more years.
options.B: Adverse possession — Sandra may claim property through long-term occupation.
options.C: Unjust enrichment — where one partner has been enriched at the expense of the other, without juristic reason, in circumstances where a corresponding deprivation has occurred; the court may remedy the unjust enrichment by imposing a constructive trust or awarding a monetary remedy.
options.D: Statutory resulting trust — Ontario family law creates an automatic resulting trust in favour of any long-term cohabiting partner.

explanation: In the absence of FLA equalization rights, a common-law partner who claims an interest in property held by the other partner must rely on the equitable doctrine of unjust enrichment as developed in Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834 and refined in Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269. Unjust enrichment requires proof of: (1) enrichment of the defendant; (2) a corresponding deprivation of the plaintiff; and (3) the absence of a juristic reason for the enrichment. If established, the court may impose a constructive trust over specific property or award a monetary remedy proportionate to the contribution.
why_A_wrong: FLA Part I equalization does not apply to common-law partners regardless of the length of cohabitation. Twelve years of cohabitation does not trigger FLA property equalization rights.
why_B_wrong: Adverse possession requires hostile, open, and continuous possession inconsistent with the true owner's title — it does not apply to domestic partners sharing a home in a consensual relationship.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Unjust enrichment (Pettkus v. Becker; Kerr v. Baranow) is the primary legal doctrine for a common-law partner's property claim upon separation; remedied by constructive trust or monetary award.
why_D_wrong: There is no statutory resulting trust created automatically for cohabiting partners in Ontario family law. Resulting trusts may arise in specific circumstances (e.g., gratuitous transfer) but are not an automatic entitlement for long-term common-law partners.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Unjust Enrichment — Basis for Common-Law Property Claim
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834; Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3

tested_concepts: three elements enrichment deprivation no juristic reason

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Elements of Unjust Enrichment — Enrichment, Deprivation, No Juristic Reason
fact_pattern: For 10 years, Patricia worked full-time and contributed 40% of her salary to the household expenses, while her common-law partner Derek paid the mortgage on his home (registered in his name alone) and invested in his business. Patricia did not do more than her fair share of household work; she paid equally for shared expenses. She now claims unjust enrichment and seeks a constructive trust over Derek's home.
call_of_question: Is Patricia likely to establish unjust enrichment entitling her to a constructive trust over Derek's home?

options.A: Yes — any contribution to household expenses by a cohabiting partner automatically establishes unjust enrichment and entitles the contributor to a constructive trust.
options.B: No — only contributions in the form of unpaid domestic labour (not monetary contributions to shared expenses) can ground an unjust enrichment claim.
options.C: Likely no — while Patricia made monetary contributions, a 50% equal sharing of household expenses does not give rise to unjust enrichment; Derek received no enrichment beyond what was bargained for in the mutual support of the household, and there is likely a juristic reason (equal contribution arrangement) negating the claim.
options.D: Yes — cohabitation for 10 years automatically establishes unjust enrichment entitling the claimant to a 50% interest in all jointly used property.

explanation: Unjust enrichment requires: (1) enrichment of the defendant; (2) a corresponding deprivation of the plaintiff; and (3) no juristic reason for the enrichment (Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269). Where Patricia contributed equally to shared household expenses (a mutual arrangement), Derek received no unjust benefit — he paid his share and Patricia paid hers. The equal contribution arrangement constitutes a juristic reason (a mutual benefit-conferring arrangement) that negates the unjust enrichment claim. Patricia's failure to show that she contributed disproportionately — beyond what was expected in a mutual sharing arrangement — makes it unlikely that the third element (no juristic reason) is met.
why_A_wrong: Paying a proportionate share of household expenses does not automatically establish unjust enrichment. The court must assess whether there is an enrichment of the defendant beyond what was agreed upon and whether there is no juristic reason for the enrichment.
why_B_wrong: Unjust enrichment can be established on the basis of monetary contributions as well as domestic labour; the form of the contribution is not limited to unpaid domestic work. However, the quality and disproportionality of the contribution matter.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Equal contribution to shared household expenses likely constitutes a juristic reason (mutual benefit arrangement), negating the third element of unjust enrichment; the claim is unlikely to succeed on these facts.
why_D_wrong: Length of cohabitation does not automatically establish unjust enrichment or a proportionate interest in property. The unjust enrichment analysis is fact-specific, not based on time alone.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Unjust Enrichment — Three Elements
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269; Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Joint Family Venture — Kerr v. Baranow Four Factors
fact_pattern: After a 15-year cohabitation during which Rose and her partner Henry jointly built a business, raised children, and pooled their incomes into a joint account, they separated. Henry's business had grown substantially in value. Rose claims a proportionate share of the business based on the concept of joint family venture. Henry argues that unjust enrichment alone governs and Rose's claim must be limited to what she can trace.
call_of_question: What is the 'joint family venture' concept articulated in Kerr v. Baranow, and how does it affect the remedy for unjust enrichment?

options.A: Joint family venture is a statutory concept under the FLA that applies to common-law couples after 10 years of cohabitation.
options.B: Joint family venture is not a recognized legal concept; unjust enrichment is limited to a monetary quantum meruit remedy calculated on an hourly wage basis.
options.C: In Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the Supreme Court recognized that where the parties engaged in a joint family venture — assessed by mutual effort, economic integration, actual intent, and priority of the family — the appropriate remedy for unjust enrichment may be a proportionate share of the accumulated wealth, rather than a strict value-survived or quantum meruit calculation.
options.D: Joint family venture creates an automatic 50/50 split of all assets accumulated during cohabitation, similar to matrimonial home rules.

explanation: In Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the Supreme Court of Canada articulated the concept of 'joint family venture' as a means of determining the appropriate remedy for unjust enrichment claims between common-law partners. Where the parties' relationship demonstrates: (1) mutual effort; (2) economic integration; (3) actual intent; and (4) priority of the family — the court may award a proportionate share of the wealth accumulated through the joint effort, rather than merely a quantum meruit (hourly wage equivalent) or strict tracing of contributions. This approach is more generous and reflects the reality of long-term cohabiting relationships that function economically like marriages.
why_A_wrong: Joint family venture is a judicial doctrine developed by the Supreme Court of Canada in Kerr v. Baranow; it is not a statutory concept under the FLA and has no duration threshold.
why_B_wrong: The Supreme Court in Kerr v. Baranow rejected the view that unjust enrichment remedies for cohabiting partners are always limited to quantum meruit. The joint family venture approach may entitle the claimant to a proportionate share of the total family wealth accumulated through mutual effort.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Kerr v. Baranow established the joint family venture doctrine assessed through four factors (mutual effort, economic integration, actual intent, priority of family), enabling a proportionate share remedy for unjust enrichment.
why_D_wrong: Joint family venture does not create an automatic 50/50 split. The remedy is proportionate to the claimant's actual contribution to the joint effort, which may be more or less than 50% depending on the circumstances.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Joint Family Venture — Kerr v. Baranow

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Spousal Support — FLA s.29 Definition of Spouse for Support Purposes
fact_pattern: Carla and Howard cohabited for 2.5 years in a conjugal relationship and separated without having any children together. Carla now seeks spousal support from Howard under the Family Law Act. Howard argues that she is not his 'spouse' for support purposes because they never married and cohabited for less than three years.
call_of_question: Under the Family Law Act s.29, is Carla entitled to seek spousal support as a common-law partner?

options.A: Yes — any person who has cohabited with another in a conjugal relationship for any period of time may seek spousal support under the FLA.
options.B: No — Howard is correct; under FLA s.29, a common-law partner must have cohabited continuously for at least three years or be the parent of a child with the other person; Carla has cohabited for only 2.5 years and they have no children together, so she does not qualify as a 'spouse' for support purposes.
options.C: Yes — because Carla is a common-law partner, she automatically qualifies as a 'spouse' under all provisions of the FLA including support.
options.D: No — common-law partners can never claim spousal support under the Family Law Act; they must proceed under the Divorce Act.

explanation: Family Law Act s.29 defines 'spouse' for the purposes of Part III (support) to include a person who has cohabited with another person continuously for a period of not less than three years, OR a person who is in a relationship of some permanence if they are the natural or adoptive parents of a child together. Carla cohabited for only 2.5 years and has no children with Howard — neither threshold in s.29 is met. Howard's argument is correct: Carla does not qualify as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 and cannot claim spousal support under the FLA. (Note: The Divorce Act applies only to married persons and is unavailable to Carla.)
why_A_wrong: FLA s.29 requires a minimum of three continuous years of cohabitation, or a relationship with a child. There is no entitlement to spousal support from any period of cohabitation regardless of length.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.29 requires three years of continuous cohabitation or parenthood of a child together; 2.5 years of cohabitation with no children falls below both thresholds.
why_C_wrong: Being a common-law partner does not automatically qualify a person as a 'spouse' under all FLA provisions. The FLA defines 'spouse' differently for different parts; for support (Part III), the threshold is three years or parenthood of a child.
why_D_wrong: The Divorce Act applies exclusively to married spouses. It is not available to common-law partners who cannot divorce because they were never married. Common-law support rights are governed by provincial legislation (FLA s.29) or the common law.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Spousal Support for Common-Law Partners — FLA s.29
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29 (definition of spouse for support purposes)

tested_concepts: FLA s.29 definition spouse for support

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Unjust Enrichment — Three Elements and Juristic Reason Categories
fact_pattern: After a 14-year common-law relationship, Nadia and Vincent separate. During the relationship, Nadia quit her job to look after their three children and Vincent's parents while Vincent built his contracting business from scratch. The home is registered in Vincent's name. Nadia claims unjust enrichment. Vincent argues he paid Nadia a weekly allowance of $200 for her household work, constituting a juristic reason for retaining the benefit.
call_of_question: Under the unjust enrichment doctrine as articulated in Kerr v. Baranow, does the payment of a modest allowance constitute a juristic reason negating Nadia's claim?

options.A: Any payment to the claimant, however modest, constitutes a complete juristic reason for the defendant's retention of the benefit.
options.B: Under Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the juristic reasons category includes: existing contracts, disposition of law, donative intent, and other policy-based reasons; a nominal $200/week payment may not fully reflect the value of Nadia's contributions and would not constitute a complete juristic reason if the payment was grossly disproportionate to the benefit conferred; the court assesses the reasonableness of the juristic reason and the degree to which it corresponds to the enrichment.
options.C: The juristic reason analysis is not applicable to common-law relationships; unjust enrichment is assessed purely on the value of the claimant's labour.
options.D: A modest payment always provides a complete juristic reason because parties are free to contract on any terms they choose.

explanation: In Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the Supreme Court confirmed that the absence of a juristic reason is an essential element of unjust enrichment. Juristic reasons include: (1) a contract; (2) a disposition of law; (3) donative intent (the claimant intended to gift the benefit); and (4) other policy reasons. A nominal payment of $200/week as an 'allowance' for extensive child-rearing and household management over 14 years may not constitute an adequate juristic reason if it is grossly disproportionate to the value of the services rendered. The court would assess whether the allowance corresponds meaningfully to the enrichment conferred. Where a payment captures only a fraction of the economic value of the contributions, it does not negate the unjust enrichment claim in full — the claim may succeed for the excess enrichment beyond the payment.
why_A_wrong: A payment does not automatically constitute a complete juristic reason. The court assesses whether the payment genuinely corresponds to the value of the benefit received. A nominal payment does not extinguish a claim for much larger contributions.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under Kerr v. Baranow, a juristic reason must adequately account for the enrichment; a nominal $200/week payment for 14 years of unpaid domestic work and child-rearing is likely insufficient to negate the full claim.
why_C_wrong: The juristic reason analysis is directly applicable to common-law relationship claims. Kerr v. Baranow itself arose from a common-law relationship; the unjust enrichment framework with the three-element test applies fully.
why_D_wrong: Freedom of contract does not mean any payment provides a complete juristic reason. The courts evaluate the proportionality of the payment to the benefit conferred. A grossly inadequate payment does not extinguish a larger unjust enrichment claim.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Unjust Enrichment — Juristic Reason Analysis
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269; Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834

tested_concepts: Kerr v Baranow juristic reason categories

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Joint Family Venture — Applying the Four Kerr v. Baranow Factors
fact_pattern: Angela and Robert cohabited for 18 years. Throughout the relationship, they each worked full-time, pooled all income into a joint account, made joint decisions about property purchases, cared for their two children together, and treated their financial lives as fully merged. Robert's investments grew significantly. On separation, Angela claims a proportionate share of all accumulated wealth.
call_of_question: Under Kerr v. Baranow, which of the four joint family venture factors are most clearly present on these facts, and what remedy does a finding of joint family venture support?

options.A: None of the four joint family venture factors are present because Angela and Robert both worked; the JFV doctrine only applies where one party worked exclusively in the home.
options.B: Under Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the four JFV factors are: (1) mutual effort; (2) economic integration; (3) actual intent; and (4) priority of the family; all four appear strongly present on these facts (pooled income, joint decisions, children, mutual full commitment); a finding of JFV supports a proportionate share remedy rather than a quantum meruit calculation.
options.C: The joint family venture doctrine entitles Angela to exactly 50% of all assets regardless of her actual contributions.
options.D: Joint family venture applies only where both parties have signed a formal partnership agreement.

explanation: In Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the Supreme Court identified four factors indicating a joint family venture: (1) mutual effort — working together toward common goals; (2) economic integration — pooling resources and finances; (3) actual intent — the parties' own understanding of their relationship as a joint enterprise; and (4) priority of the family — decisions made in the interests of the family unit. On the facts, all four factors are strongly present: 18 years of cohabitation, pooled income, joint property decisions, raising children together, and fully merged financial lives. A finding of JFV supports a proportionate share remedy for the unjust enrichment claim — Angela would receive a share proportional to her actual contribution to the jointly acquired wealth, which may be close to 50% given the full economic integration.
why_A_wrong: The JFV doctrine is not limited to situations where one party worked solely at home. The Supreme Court in Kerr v. Baranow applied the doctrine to relationships where both parties worked and pooled resources. The relevant question is whether they functioned as a joint economic enterprise.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. All four Kerr v. Baranow JFV factors are present; the remedy is a proportionate share of accumulated wealth from the joint venture.
why_C_wrong: A JFV finding does not automatically produce a 50% split. The remedy is proportionate to Angela's actual contribution to the joint venture — which may be more or less than 50%, determined by the court on all the evidence.
why_D_wrong: A joint family venture is a judicially determined equitable concept, not a formal partnership. No written partnership agreement is required. The court assesses the substance of the relationship using the four factors.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Joint Family Venture — Four Kerr v. Baranow Factors

tested_concepts: four JFV factors Kerr v Baranow
all four factors can be present with two earners
JFV not limited to one partner at home

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Presumption of Resulting Trust — Gratuitous Transfer Between Partners
fact_pattern: During their common-law relationship, Craig transferred $80,000 from his personal savings account to an investment account he registered solely in his partner Leigh's name, without any written explanation. Three years later, they separate. Craig claims the money was always his and that Leigh holds it on a resulting trust for him. Leigh argues it was a gift to her.
call_of_question: What is the presumption of resulting trust and how does it apply to Craig's $80,000 transfer to Leigh?

options.A: In a common-law relationship, any transfer of money between partners is presumed to be a gift; Craig bears the burden of proving otherwise.
options.B: Under the law of resulting trusts, where one person gratuitously transfers property to another person with whom they are not married, there is a presumption of resulting trust — the recipient holds the property on trust for the transferor; Craig does not need to prove the money was a loan; Leigh bears the burden of rebutting the presumption by proving Craig intended a gift.
options.C: The presumption of resulting trust applies only to transfers of real property, not to money transfers.
options.D: The presumption of resulting trust applies only between spouses, not between common-law partners.

explanation: The presumption of resulting trust arises at common law when a person gratuitously transfers property to another person who is not their spouse. In such cases, the law presumes the recipient holds the property on a resulting trust for the transferor — the transferor's intent is presumed to be to retain beneficial ownership. The recipient bears the burden of rebutting this presumption by showing the transferor intended an outright gift. Note that for married spouses, the presumption of advancement applies in the opposite direction (a gratuitous transfer between spouses is presumed a gift). Craig and Leigh are not married, so the resulting trust presumption applies to the $80,000 transfer; Leigh must prove Craig intended a gift to rebut it.
why_A_wrong: The presumption of advancement (gift) applies between spouses (married) or from parent to child. It does not automatically apply between common-law partners. For non-spousal, non-parent-child gratuitous transfers, the presumption of resulting trust applies.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The presumption of resulting trust applies to gratuitous transfers between non-spouses; Leigh bears the burden of proving donative intent to rebut the presumption.
why_C_wrong: The presumption of resulting trust applies to all types of gratuitous transfers — money, real property, personal property — not only to real estate transfers. The $80,000 cash transfer attracts the presumption.
why_D_wrong: The presumption of resulting trust applies between non-spouses, including common-law partners. It is the presumption of advancement (the opposite presumption, toward gift) that applies between married spouses. Common-law partners attract the resulting trust presumption for gratuitous transfers.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Presumption of Resulting Trust — Gratuitous Transfers
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Pecore v. Pecore, [2007] 1 SCR 795; Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269

tested_concepts: presumption of advancement for married spouses
recipient bears burden to prove gift
applies to all types of property

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Definition of Common-Law Spouse Under FLA
subtopic_heading: FLA s.29 Definition — Cohabited 3 Years Continuously or Parent of Child
fact_pattern: David and Isabella cohabited in a conjugal relationship for 3.5 years. During this time, they had no children together. They now separate and Isabella seeks spousal support. David argues she does not qualify as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 because they were not married and she must establish an independent ground.
call_of_question: Under FLA s.29, does Isabella qualify as a 'spouse' for the purpose of spousal support?

options.A: No — FLA s.29 requires five years of continuous cohabitation; 3.5 years is insufficient.
options.B: Yes — under FLA s.29, 'spouse' for Part III (support) purposes includes a person who has cohabited with another person in a conjugal relationship continuously for at least three years; Isabella satisfies this threshold and may seek spousal support.
options.C: No — a person who has not been married cannot be a 'spouse' under any FLA provision.
options.D: Yes — any cohabitation in a conjugal relationship, regardless of duration, qualifies under FLA s.29.

explanation: Family Law Act s.29 defines 'spouse' for the purposes of Part III (support obligations) to include a person who has cohabited with another person in a conjugal relationship continuously for a period of not less than three years. Isabella and David cohabited continuously for 3.5 years in a conjugal relationship — meeting the three-year threshold. Accordingly, Isabella qualifies as a 'spouse' under s.29 and has standing to apply for spousal support under the FLA. The threshold under s.29 is three years of continuous cohabitation OR parenthood of a child together; the marriage threshold does not apply for FLA support purposes.
why_A_wrong: FLA s.29 requires a minimum of three years of continuous cohabitation, not five. At 3.5 years, Isabella exceeds the three-year threshold and qualifies as a 'spouse' for FLA Part III support purposes.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.29 includes persons who have cohabited in a conjugal relationship for at least three continuous years; Isabella's 3.5 years satisfies the threshold.
why_C_wrong: FLA s.29 expressly extends the definition of 'spouse' for support purposes to include persons who have not been married, provided they meet the cohabitation threshold. Unmarried persons can qualify as a 'spouse' under FLA Part III.
why_D_wrong: FLA s.29 requires at least three years of continuous cohabitation for unmarried persons. Any cohabitation, however brief, does not qualify. The three-year minimum (or parenthood of a child) must be met.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: FLA s.29 — Definition of Spouse for Support
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Domestic Violence Remedies for Unmarried Partners
subtopic_heading: Domestic Violence — Injunctive Relief and Protection Orders for Unmarried Partners
fact_pattern: Margaret and Eric have cohabited for 2 years without marrying. Eric has become increasingly violent toward Margaret. She wants to seek an order restraining Eric from the home and from having any contact with her. Eric argues that the exclusive possession and restraining order provisions under the FLA apply only to married spouses and Margaret cannot access these protections.
call_of_question: What legal remedies are available to Margaret as an unmarried common-law partner facing domestic violence?

options.A: Eric is correct; FLA exclusive possession and restraining orders are only available to married spouses; Margaret has no family law remedies and must rely solely on criminal law.
options.B: Unmarried cohabiting partners have access to protection orders and restraining orders under the FLA for persons who meet the definition of 'spouse'; for those who do not meet the threshold, the Family Law Act and the Domestic Violence Protection Act (DVPA) still provide mechanisms, and a restraining order under FLA s.46 is available to persons who have cohabited; a peace bond or emergency protection order may also be sought.
options.C: Margaret must first obtain a marriage certificate before she can access any family law protective orders.
options.D: Margaret can only access criminal law remedies (peace bond, assault charges); family law protection orders are unavailable to unmarried partners in Ontario.

explanation: Ontario provides multiple protective mechanisms for persons in intimate relationships who are at risk of domestic violence, regardless of marital status. The Family Law Act s.46 provides that a court may make an order restraining a person from molesting, annoying, or harassing the applicant or children in their lawful custody. Restraining orders under s.46 are available to persons who have cohabited even if they do not meet the three-year FLA s.29 spousal support threshold. Additionally, Ontario's Family Court may grant protective orders under the Children's Law Reform Act and the Family Law Rules. Criminal law remedies (peace bonds, no-contact conditions as bail conditions, assault charges) are also available. While the FLA exclusive possession regime (Part II) technically applies to 'spouses' (which for Part II purposes is limited to married spouses), other provisions and statutes provide meaningful protection for unmarried partners.
why_A_wrong: Unmarried cohabiting partners are not without family law remedies. FLA s.46 restraining orders, criminal peace bonds, and other protective mechanisms are available regardless of marital status. Eric's argument is incorrect.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.46 restraining orders are available to persons who have cohabited; peace bonds and other criminal remedies supplement these; married status is not required for all protective orders.
why_C_wrong: A marriage certificate is not required to access protective orders. FLA s.46 and criminal law remedies are available to cohabiting partners regardless of marital status.
why_D_wrong: Family law protection orders — specifically restraining orders under FLA s.46 — are available to unmarried cohabiting partners. Margaret is not limited to criminal law remedies only.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Domestic Violence — Remedies for Unmarried Partners
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 46; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29

tested_concepts: FLA s.46 restraining order available to cohabiting partners
criminal and family law remedies concurrent
